text:
Nitride phosphors are essential components for the advancement of white light-emitting diode (w-LED) technologies. In this study, we demonstrate that the luminescent properties of these phosphors can be significantly enhanced through strategic cation substitution, which induces structural modifications, charge variations, and a rearrangement of neighboring nitride clusters. By substituting Ca²⁺ with Sr²⁺, we observed an expansion of the lattice volume and the $bc$ plane, contrasted by a contraction along the $a$-axis. This lattice distortion creates high-coordination environments and sufficient spatial volume around the Sr/Eu centers, facilitating the effective reduction and charge stabilization of activators, as confirmed by X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy.

These structural refinements resulted in a greater than 10% increase in photoluminescent intensity alongside a notable blue shift. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was employed to characterize the microstructural evolution, while solid-state Raman spectroscopy revealed unique shifts in the anion environment following cation substitution. Furthermore, solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy identified specific ordering variations within the SiN₄ and AlN₄ tetrahedra surrounding the divalent centers. This localized structural ordering contributes to a more rigid framework, significantly improving thermal robustness. Specifically, the $Sr_xCa_{0.993-x}AlSiN_3:Eu^{2+}_{0.007}$ phosphor (at $x = 0.9$) exhibited a 10% improvement in thermal stability at 473 K compared to its Sr-free counterpart. These findings highlight the critical role of neighbor-sequence engineering in tetrahedral sites—coordinated by elements such as Li, Mg, Al, and Si—for the design of high-performance phosphors.
